title: STANDARD FOR TREE TOMATOES 1 CXS 303-2011 Adopted in 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR TREE TOMATOES 1 CXS 303-2011 Adopted in 2011.
sections.summary: Document title and identifier: 'Standard for Tree Tomatoes (CXS 303-2011)', adopted 2011, noting the commodity is commonly known in some regions as 'tamarillo'.

Defines the scope as commercial varieties of tree tomatoes grown from Cyphomandra betacea Sendt or Solanum betaceum Cav. for supply fresh to the consumer and explicitly excludes tree tomatoes intended for industrial processing.

Sets the quality regime: specifies minimum quality and maturity/transport requirements (e.g., whole, sound, clean, free of pests, firm, stalk present), and establishes three quality classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with differing permitted superficial defects and skin defect area limits, none of which may affect the pulp.

Lists minimum requirements applicable to all classes: fruit must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and visible foreign matter, free of abnormal external moisture, foreign smell or taste, firm, fresh in appearance, with the stalk present to the first knot, and adequately developed/ripened to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

Classifies tree tomatoes into three classes—'Extra' (superior quality, virtually defect‑free), Class I (good quality allowing slight shape and skin defects up to 10% surface area without pulp damage), and Class II (for produce meeting minimum requirements but not higher classes, allowing greater defects including skin defects up to 20% surface area without pulp damage).

'Extra' Class: fruit must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or package presentation.

Class I: fruit must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety, with only slight allowable defects (e.g., shape faults or skin scratches/blemishes not exceeding 10% of total surface) provided the pulp is unaffected and overall presentation is preserved.

Class II: includes fruit not meeting higher classes but satisfying minimum requirements, permitting defects such as shape faults (e.g., apex extension/flattening) and skin scratches/blemishes up to 20% of surface area, provided the pulp remains unaffected.

Permits sizing by diameter (maximum equatorial diameter with codes A–E defined by millimetre ranges), by weight (grams with size codes 1–4), or by count (number of fruit per package) with corresponding size code tables.

Allows tolerances by package for produce not meeting the declared class or size, specifying quantitative allowances for quality (Extra: 5%; Class I: 10%; Class II: 10% for produce not meeting class or minimum requirements, excluding rotting) and a 10% size tolerance by number or weight for fruit one size above and/or below the declared size.

Details quality tolerances by class: Extra allows 5% by number/weight not meeting Extra but meeting Class I; Class I allows 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II; Class II allows 10% not meeting the class or minimum requirements, excluding produce unfit for consumption.
Extra class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of fruit in the package may fail to meet 'Extra' requirements provided they meet Class I or, exceptionally, the tolerances of Class I.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of fruit in the package may fail to meet Class I requirements provided they meet Class II or, exceptionally, the tolerances of Class II.

Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of fruit may fail to meet Class II requirements or the minimum requirements, except that produce affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption is excluded.
Size tolerances: for all classes, up to 10% by number or weight of fruit in a package may correspond to the size immediately above and/or below the size indicated on the package.

Presentation requirements: packages must be uniform (same origin, variety, quality, colour and size) with the visible contents representative; maturity may be gauged visually and by flesh examination/iodine test. Packaging must protect produce using new, clean materials that do not damage fruit, use non‑toxic inks/glues, comply with CXC 44-1995, and containers must meet hygiene, ventilation and resistance criteria and be free of foreign matter and smells.
Requires each package to contain uniform tree tomatoes of the same origin, variety, quality, colour and size, and that the visible portion of the package be representative of the entire contents.

Requires tree tomatoes to be packed to protect the produce using new, clean materials that do not cause damage and permits printed materials if non-toxic; packaging must comply with the Codex Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of tree tomatoes, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Adds specific labelling provisions in addition to General Standard CXS 1-1985: consumer-package rules for naming produce and optional variety labelling, and non-retail container rules requiring particular particulars legibly and indelibly on one side or in accompanying documents. Non-retail particulars include identification, nature and origin of produce, commercial identification (class, size, optional net weight) and an optional official inspection mark.

States that consumer packages must meet General Standard CXS 1-1985 and, if the produce is not visible externally, the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety name.
If the produce is not visible from the outside, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may be labelled with the variety.

Non-retail containers must bear, legibly and indelibly on the same side or in accompanying documents, required particulars including identification (exporter/packer/dispatcher), nature of produce, origin, commercial identification (class, size, optional net weight) and an optional official inspection mark.
Requires the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher to be on the package, with an optional identification code.
Requires the name of the produce on the package if the contents are not visible from the outside.
Requires declaration of the country of origin and optionally the district or national/regional/local place name.
Requires commercial identification on packages: class, size (code, diameter, weight range or count) and optional net weight.
Heading for an optional Official Inspection Mark; the section contains no substantive requirements beyond indicating the mark is optional.
Mandates that the produce comply with Codex maximum levels for contaminants and toxins (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex hygienic codes, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997; notes that recycled materials are acceptable if food-grade and that some national laws require explicit name/address declarations when code marks are used.
